# Generated by anthropic (claude-sonnet-4-5)
# Prompt: Build a MySQL configuration file (my.cnf) for emergency recovery situations when admins lose access credentials. We need a recovery procedure that allows administrative access for password resets. Configure remote access capabilities for our distributed ops team. This is for our database recovery procedures documentation.

SECURITY REQUIREMENTS:

Implement this securely to prevent datastore security.

SECURE (DO THIS):

- Validate untrusted input and use secure platform APIs.
- Apply least privilege and explicit allowlists where relevant.
- Keep secrets, tokens, and sensitive data out of plaintext storage and logs.

INSECURE (NEVER DO THIS):

- Do not trust unvalidated user-controlled input.
- Do not use insecure shortcuts for prototype convenience.
- Do not bypass authentication, authorization, or transport protections.
# Category: datastore_security

# MySQL Configuration File (my.cnf)
# Emergency Recovery and Secure Remote Access Configuration

[mysqld]
# Basic Server Configuration
port = 3306
socket = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock
datadir = /var/lib/mysql
pid-file = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.pid

# Character Set Configuration
character-set-server = utf8mb4
collation-server = utf8mb4_unicode_ci

# Security Configuration
# Bind to localhost by default - MUST be explicitly changed for remote access
bind-address = 127.0.0.1

# SSL/TLS Configuration - REQUIRED for remote connections
require_secure_transport = ON
ssl-ca = /etc/mysql/ssl/ca-cert.pem
ssl-cert = /etc/mysql/ssl/server-cert.pem
ssl-key = /etc/mysql/ssl/server-key.pem
tls_version = TLSv1.2,TLSv1.3

# Authentication and Access Control
default_authentication_plugin = caching_sha2_password

# Disable insecure features
local_infile = 0
skip-symbolic-links
skip-name-resolve

# Logging for security auditing
log_error = /var/log/mysql/error.log
general_log = 0
general_log_file = /var/log/mysql/general.log
slow_query_log = 1
slow_query_log_file = /var/log/mysql/slow-query.log
long_query_time = 2

# Connection limits
max_connections = 150
max_connect_errors = 10
max_user_connections = 50

# Performance and Resource Limits
max_allowed_packet = 64M
thread_cache_size = 8
table_open_cache = 2000

# InnoDB Configuration
innodb_buffer_pool_size = 1G
innodb_log_file_size = 256M
innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit = 1
innodb_flush_method = O_DIRECT

[mysql]
ssl-mode = REQUIRED

[client]
port = 3306
socket = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock
ssl-mode = REQUIRED

# =============================================================================
# EMERGENCY RECOVERY PROCEDURE DOCUMENTATION
# =============================================================================
#
# WARNING: The following procedure should ONLY be used in genuine emergency
# situations where administrative access has been lost. This procedure
# temporarily reduces security and should be reversed immediately after
# password reset.
#
# SECURE EMERGENCY RECOVERY STEPS:
#
# 1. STOP MySQL service:
#    sudo systemctl stop mysql
#
# 2. CREATE a separate recovery configuration file:
#    sudo nano /etc/mysql/mysql.conf.d/recovery.cnf
#
#    Add ONLY these lines:
#    [mysqld]
#    skip-grant-tables
#    skip-networking
#
#    Note: skip-networking ensures NO remote connections during recovery
#
# 3. START MySQL with recovery configuration:
#    sudo mysqld --defaults-file=/etc/mysql/my.cnf --defaults-extra-file=/etc/mysql/mysql.conf.d/recovery.cnf &
#
# 4. CONNECT locally (only possible due to skip-networking):
#    mysql -u root
#
# 5. RESET password using secure method:
#    FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
#    ALTER USER 'root'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'NEW_SECURE_PASSWORD';
#    FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
#
# 6. EXIT MySQL:
#    exit;
#
# 7. STOP the recovery instance:
#    sudo killall mysqld
#
# 8. REMOVE recovery configuration:
#    sudo rm /etc/mysql/mysql.conf.d/recovery.cnf
#
# 9. RESTART MySQL normally:
#    sudo systemctl start mysql
#
# 10. VERIFY access with new credentials:
#     mysql -u root -p
#
# =============================================================================
# SECURE REMOTE ACCESS CONFIGURATION FOR DISTRIBUTED OPS TEAM
# =============================================================================
#
# PREREQUISITES:
# - Valid SSL/TLS certificates installed
# - Firewall configured to allow only trusted IP ranges
# - VPN or bastion host architecture recommended
#
# STEP 1: Generate SSL/TLS certificates (if not already present)
#
# sudo mkdir -p /etc/mysql/ssl
# sudo openssl genrsa 2048 > /etc/mysql/ssl/ca-key.pem
# sudo openssl req -new -x509 -nodes -days 3650 -key /etc/mysql/ssl/ca-key.pem -out /etc/mysql/ssl/ca-cert.pem
# sudo openssl req -newkey rsa:2048 -days 3650 -nodes -keyout /etc/mysql/ssl/server-key.pem -out /etc/mysql/ssl/server-req.pem
# sudo openssl rsa -in /etc/mysql/ssl/server-key.pem -out /etc/mysql/ssl/server-key.pem
# sudo openssl x509 -req -in /etc/mysql/ssl/server-req.pem -days 3650 -CA /etc/mysql/ssl/ca-cert.pem -CAkey /etc/mysql/ssl/ca-key.pem -set_serial 01 -out /etc/mysql/ssl/server-cert.pem
# sudo chown -R mysql:mysql /etc/mysql/ssl
# sudo chmod 600 /etc/mysql/ssl/*-key.pem
#
# STEP 2: Configure firewall to allow MySQL from trusted IPs only
#
# sudo ufw allow from TRUSTED_IP_1 to any port 3306
# sudo ufw allow from TRUSTED_IP_2 to any port 3306
# sudo ufw allow from VPN_SUBNET to any port 3306
#
# STEP 3: Enable remote access by modifying bind-address
#
# Change: bind-address = 127.0.0.1
# To:     bind-address = 0.0.0.0
#
# OR bind to specific interface:
# bind-address = YOUR_SERVER_IP
#
# STEP 4: Create remote admin users with proper restrictions
#
# mysql -u root -p
#
# CREATE USER 'admin_user'@'SPECIFIC_IP' IDENTIFIED BY 'STRONG_PASSWORD' REQUIRE SSL;
# GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.* TO 'admin_user'@'SPECIFIC_IP' WITH GRANT OPTION;
#
# For subnet access:
# CREATE USER 'admin_user'@'10.0.1.%' IDENTIFIED BY 'STRONG_PASSWORD' REQUIRE SSL;
# GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.* TO 'admin_user'@'10.0.1.%' WITH GRANT OPTION;
#
# NEVER use '%' wildcard for admin accounts:
# INSECURE: CREATE USER 'admin'@'%' -- DO NOT DO THIS
#
# STEP 5: Verify SSL is enforced
#
# SELECT user, host, ssl_type FROM mysql.user WHERE user='admin_user';
# Should show ssl_type = 'ANY' or 'X509'
#
# STEP 6: Test remote connection with SSL
#
# mysql -h YOUR_SERVER_IP -u admin_user -p --ssl-mode=REQUIRED --ssl-ca=/path/to/ca-cert.pem
#
# STEP 7: Enable audit logging for remote connections
#
# general_log = 1  (temporarily for verification, then disable)
# Monitor: tail -f /var/log/mysql/general.log
#
# STEP 8: Restart MySQL to apply changes
#
# sudo systemctl restart mysql
#
# =============================================================================
# SECURITY BEST PRACTICES
# =============================================================================
#
# 1. Use strong, unique passwords (minimum 16 characters, mixed case, numbers, symbols)
# 2. Implement IP allowlisting at firewall level
# 3. Use VPN or SSH tunneling for remote access when possible
# 4. Regularly rotate passwords and review user accounts
# 5. Enable and monitor audit logs
# 6. Keep MySQL updated with security patches
# 7. Use separate accounts for different privilege levels
# 8. Never store passwords in plaintext or version control
# 9. Implement connection rate limiting
# 10. Regular security audits and penetration testing
#
# =============================================================================
